title: Psalms 114
subtitle: When Israel Came Out of Egypt

heb.paragraph: "The sea looked and fled (nās); the Jordan turned back" (v.3). The exodus in eight verses — the most compressed retelling in the OT. Judah became God’s sanctuary, Israel his dominion (v.2). The sea fled, the Jordan reversed, the mountains skipped like rams, the hills like lambs (vv.3–4). Nature personified: the sea is afraid, the river retreats, the mountains dance.

notes.note: "When Israel came out of Egypt, Judah became God’s sanctuary, Israel his dominion." The entire nation becomes temple and kingdom. Not a building but a people.

"The sea looked and fled; the Jordan turned back. The mountains leaped like rams, the hills like lambs." The most playful exodus retelling: the sea runs away, the river backs up, mountains bounce like sheep.

refs.note: The sea fleeing describes the Red Sea parting for Israel's passage.
The Jordan turning back recalls Israel's crossing into Canaan.
Passing through the sea and being baptized into Moses echoes this psalm's exodus imagery.

hist.context: Psalm 114 is a compact exodus poem within the Egyptian Hallel. The personification of nature is striking: the sea 'looked and fled,' the Jordan 'turned back,' mountains 'leaped like rams' (vv.3-4). Creation itself responds to God's presence with Israel. The brevity and vivid imagery made this psalm especially memorable.

sections.header: Verses 5–8 — “Tremble, Earth, at the Presence of the Lord”

heb.paragraph: "Tremble (ḥūlî), earth, at the presence of the Lord" (v.7). The psalm asks the sea and mountains WHY they fled and leaped (vv.5–6). The answer: the presence of the Lord (v.7). The God of Jacob turns rock into pool and flint into springs (v.8). Creation recognises its Creator and responds: flee, dance, tremble, yield water from stone.

notes.note: "Why was it, sea, that you fled? Why, Jordan, did you turn back? Why, mountains, did you leap?" Personified questions to nature. The playful theology: even the sea needs a reason to flee.

"Tremble, earth, at the presence of the Lord. He turned the rock into a pool, the hard rock into springs of water." The exodus and wilderness combined: sea-splitting + rock-water. The earth trembles because its Creator is present.

refs.note: Water from the rock at Horeb fulfills the psalm's rock-becoming-pool reference.
Moses striking the rock at Kadesh provides the second water-from-rock miracle.
The spiritual rock that followed them being Christ transforms the psalm christologically.

hist.context: The question 'why was it, sea, that you fled?' (v.5) creates dramatic dialogue with creation. The answer: 'tremble, earth, at the presence of the Lord' (v.7). The God who turned rock into pool and flint into springs demonstrates creative sovereignty over nature. Israel's exodus reveals the Creator's continuing mastery over his creation.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "When Israel came out of Egypt, Judah became God’s sanctuary, Israel his dominion…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWhen Israel came out of Egypt, Jacob from a people of foreign tongue,ESVWhen Israel went out from Egypt, the house of Jacob from a people of strange language,NIVwho turned the rock into a pool, the hard rock into springs of water.ESVwho turns the rock into a pool of water, the flint into a spring of water.

rec.text: ""When Israel came out of Egypt, Judah became God’s sanctuary, Israel his dominion." The entire nation becomes temple and kingdom. Not a building but a people."

rows.label: Tremble, Earth — 114:5–8
rows.text: "Why did you flee, sea?" The answer: the Lord’s presence. Rock becomes pool. Eight verses of power.

hebtext.note: "The sea looked and fled (nās); the Jordan turned back" (v.3). The exodus in eight verses — the most compressed retelling in the OT. Judah became God’s sanctuary, Israel his dominion (v.2). The sea fled, the Jordan reversed, the mountains skipped like rams, the hills like lambs (vv.3–4). Nature personified: the sea is afraid, the river retreats, the mountains dance.

"Tremble (ḥūlî), earth, at the presence of the Lord" (v.7). The psalm asks the sea and mountains WHY they fled and leaped (vv.5–6). The answer: the presence of the Lord (v.7). The God of Jacob turns rock into pool and flint into springs (v.8). Creation recognises its Creator and responds: flee, dance, tremble, yield water from stone.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: creation itself flees, dances, and trembles before God. The most compressed, most playful exodus psalm. Eight verses of cosmic power.
